brand.name: RankYourPolitician
brand.tagline: Know your leaders. Hold them accountable.

central.title: Government of India
central.subtitle: The top of the ladder: the Prime Minister and Union ministers who run India's national departments. Start here, then go down to your state, district and area.
central.comingSoon: Central government data is being added.

hierarchy.title: How power flows in India
hierarchy.help: Start at the top and go down to find who is responsible at each level.

hierarchyPage.title: The org chart of India
hierarchyPage.subtitle: One simple chart of who answers to whom - from you, the voter, down to your local office. Tap any level to explore the real people in it.
hierarchyPage.youDesc: In a democracy the chart starts with you. Every elected person below works for you, and every appointed officer works under someone you elected.
hierarchyPage.parliamentDesc: Makes national laws. You directly elect your Lok Sabha MP; state legislators choose Rajya Sabha MPs.
hierarchyPage.electsPresident: MPs + state MLAs elect the Head of State
hierarchyPage.presidentAppoints: Appoints the PM who commands the Lok Sabha's confidence
hierarchyPage.pmDesc: Run the country day to day - defence, railways, highways, taxes, telecom. Each minister heads a national department.
hierarchyPage.stateDesc: You elect your MLA; the majority picks the Chief Minister. States run police, schools, hospitals, state roads and water.
hierarchyPage.districtDesc: The Collector / District Magistrate (DM) and the Superintendent of Police (SP) - appointed officers who deliver services and safety in each district.
hierarchyPage.localDesc: Your councillor, sarpanch and ward officers - garbage, drains, local roads, street lights and certificates.
hierarchyPage.judiciaryDesc: Courts (Supreme Court → High Courts → district courts) are independent of everyone above. They answer to the Constitution, not to ministers - that is what keeps the chart honest.
howToItems: Local problem (garbage, drains, streetlight)? Start at the bottom - your local body or ward office.
State subject (police, government school or hospital, state road)? Your MLA and the state government.
National subject (railways, highways, income tax, passports)? Your MP and the Union ministry.
Not solved? Move ONE level up the chart - that is exactly what escalation means.

finder.title: Facing a problem? Find who's responsible
finder.subtitle: Pick your problem to see which office is in charge, how to complain, and how to escalate - plus the elected leader you can also press.
finder.orderNote: Approach in this order - start at the top.
finder.findPerson: Find the officer for your area
finder.findPersonHelp: Open your district to see the current Collector / SP where we have them.
finder.parallelCouncillor: You can also raise it with your local councillor or MLA.
finder.parallelPanchayat: You can also raise it with your Gram Panchayat or MLA.
finder.chooseAbove: Choose a problem above to see who's responsible and how to escalate.
finder.learnTiers: New to this? Learn who does what
finder.disclaimer: General guidance based on how Indian administration usually works. Exact office names vary by state.
finder.escalationTitle: The full escalation ladder - who is above them

collector_dm.label: District Collector / DM
collector_dm.handles: Revenue, land, certificates, disaster relief and overall district coordination - the safe catch-all for your area.
collector_dm.escalate: The top district authority. Escalate here when a department does not respond.

sp_district.label: Superintendent of Police (SP)
sp_district.handles: All policing, safety and FIRs in the district.
sp_district.escalate: Police station (the SHO, the officer in charge) → DSP / Circle Officer → Superintendent of Police (SP) → state police chief (DGP). If the station won't file your FIR, a magistrate (judge) can order it - the power is in BNSS Section 175(3).

mun_commissioner.label: Municipal Commissioner
mun_commissioner.handles: All city civic services - roads, water, garbage, drains, lights, property tax.
mun_commissioner.escalate: Ward Officer → Deputy/Zonal Commissioner → Commissioner / grievance cell.

ward_officer.label: Ward Officer / Ward Engineer
ward_officer.handles: Your first stop in a city for potholes, garbage, drains and street lights.
ward_officer.escalate: Start here, then escalate to the Municipal Commissioner.

cdo_ceozp.label: Rural development head (CEO, Zila Parishad / CDO)
cdo_ceozp.handles: Rural development works and government-scheme delivery.
cdo_ceozp.escalate: Block Development Officer (BDO) → CEO of the Zila Parishad (the district rural-development council) → District Collector.

cmo_health.label: Chief Medical Officer (CMO)
cmo_health.handles: Government hospitals, local health centres (PHCs), vaccinations and ambulances.
cmo_health.escalate: Local health-centre doctor (PHC Medical Officer) → Block Medical Officer → Chief Medical Officer (CMO) → Director, Health Services.

deo_education.label: District Education Officer
deo_education.handles: Government schools, teachers, the Right to Education (RTE) and mid-day meals.
deo_education.escalate: Head Teacher → Block Education Officer → DEO → Director, School Education.

ee_pwd.label: Executive Engineer, Public Works Dept (PWD)
ee_pwd.handles: State and district roads and public buildings.
ee_pwd.escalate: Junior Engineer → Assistant Engineer → Executive Engineer → Chief Engineer.

ee_phed.label: Executive Engineer, Water (PHED)
ee_phed.handles: Rural piped water supply (Jal Jeevan Mission).
ee_phed.escalate: Junior Engineer → Assistant Engineer → Executive Engineer → Chief Engineer.

dso_pds.label: District Supply Officer
dso_pds.escalate: Fair Price Shop → Supply Inspector → District Supply Officer → Collector.

bdo.label: Block Development Officer (BDO)
bdo.handles: Rural works, the rural job-guarantee scheme (MGNREGA) and rural housing, at the block level.
bdo.escalate: Gram Panchayat → BDO → CEO Zila Parishad → Collector.

tehsildar_sdm.label: Tehsildar / SDM (Sub-Divisional Magistrate)
tehsildar_sdm.handles: Land records, updating who owns a property after a sale or inheritance (mutation), and certificates - at the tehsil / sub-division office.
tehsildar_sdm.escalate: Village land-record officer (Patwari) → Tehsildar → SDM → Collector.

panchayat_secretary.label: Gram Panchayat Secretary
panchayat_secretary.handles: Village-level civic works, alongside the elected Sarpanch.

discom.label: Electricity Board (DISCOM)
discom.escalate: Use the DISCOM helpline or app: Junior Engineer (JE) → Assistant / Executive Engineer of your division.

nhai.label: NHAI / National Highways
nhai.escalate: Raise via the national grievance portal (CPGRAMS).

chief_secretary.label: Chief Secretary
chief_secretary.handles: The state's top bureaucrat - final administrative escalation.
chief_secretary.escalate: Use after the district and department have failed to respond.

dgp.label: Director General of Police (DGP)
dgp.handles: Head of the state police - final police escalation.
dgp.escalate: Use after the SP has not acted.

cabinet_secretary.label: Cabinet Secretary
cabinet_secretary.handles: The Union government's top bureaucrat (coordinates ministries). Not a citizen contact point.

officials.title: Officials for this district
officials.subtitle: The appointed officers who run day-to-day services here. Information only - these officers are not rated.
officials.currentlyUnknown: We don't name this officer - postings change often. Use the district's own directory below, which is kept current:
officials.currentlyUnknownBare: We don't name this officer - postings change often. Search your district's official website for its "Who's Who" directory, which is kept current.
officials.findCta: Have a specific problem? Find who's responsible
officials.chainTitle: Reporting chain - who this office answers to
officials.chainIntro: Where this office sits, and who to approach if it does not act. This is the chain of appointed officials; your elected MLA and MP are a separate, parallel lever.

search.placeholder: Search a leader, area, district or party…
search.title: Search
search.noResults: No matches. Try a name, area, district or party.
search.hint: Try a place like Goa, an area like Mandi, or a leader's name.
search.error: Search could not load. Check your connection and try again.

home.heroSubtitle: Find who represents your area, what they are responsible for, and how they are doing - with proof from official records.
home.creed: In Democracy, people should lead and leaders should follow.
home.exploreHelp: Every state and UT, one tap away.
home.mapHint: Tap any state to meet its leaders
home.mapAria: Interactive map of India. Select a state to view its representatives.
home.topHelp: Ranked by their official work record. This is information, not a verdict.
home.perfHelp: How they perform - from official government records.
home.ratingHelp: What people think - from your ratings.
home.tiersHelp: Different problems need different leaders. Tap to see who to ask.

state.subtitle: Everyone who represents {state} - the state government, MPs in Delhi, MLAs in the assembly, and every district.
state.mapHelp: Tap a district to see its MPs, MLAs and officials. Darker = more representatives linked.
state.mapAria: District map of {state}. Select a district to view its representatives.
state.compositionHelp: Seats held in the Vidhan Sabha, from our tracked MLAs. Factual count - not a rating.
state.districtsHelp: Every district, with how many representatives we track there.
state.hierarchyCta: See where {state} sits in the org chart of India

district.subtitle: Your representatives and officials in {district} - who they are, what they handle, and how to escalate.
district.repsHelp: The people this district votes for. Tap anyone to see their record, responsibilities and how to hold them accountable.
district.noReps: We haven't linked representatives to this district yet - check the constituencies below.
district.mapHelp: This district inside {state}. Tap a neighbouring district to jump to it.
district.areasHelp: The Lok Sabha and assembly seats that cover this district.
district.whoHelp: Pick a problem to see the actual people responsible - from the local office up to the Chief Minister.
district.whoCta: Open the full finder with escalation steps

area.explainPc: This area elects one Member of Parliament (MP) to the Lok Sabha in New Delhi - your voice in national law-making.
area.explainAc: This area elects one Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) to the state assembly - your voice on police, schools, hospitals and state roads.
area.explainRs: Rajya Sabha members are elected by state legislators, not directly by voters, and represent the whole state in Parliament's upper house.
area.explainMlc: Legislative Council members are chosen by MLAs, local bodies, graduates and teachers - they review state laws in the upper house.
area.repHelp: The person this area elected. See their full record, scores and responsibilities.
area.noRep: No sitting representative on record for this seat right now.
area.siblingsHelp: Other seats that share a district with this one.
area.mapHelp: The exact boundary of this seat inside {state}.
area.mapAria: Map showing the {name} constituency highlighted within {state}
area.districtsHelp: The civil district(s) this seat falls in - where the DM/Collector and SP for this area sit.

india.compositionHelp: Seats held by each party among the MPs we track. Factual count - not a rating.
india.constitutionalHelp: The offices the government answers to: the Head of State, the presiding officers of Parliament, and the statutory Leaders of the Opposition. Informational - never rated.

who.locateHelp: Pick your district to see the actual people responsible near you - not just the office names.
who.pickForPeople: Pick your district above to see the real people responsible - with names, photos and contacts.
who.stepLevel1: Level 1 - start here, in your area
who.stepLevel2: Level 2 - block / tehsil, if no action
who.stepMla: Your MLA - your area's elected leader
who.pickAc: Which assembly constituency (area) are you in?
who.mlaEscalateHint: Your MLA can push every office above and raise your issue in the Assembly - go to them before any minister.
who.titlesVary: Exact office titles vary by state - the order of levels is what matters.
who.stepMinister: Last resort - the state minister who runs this department
who.noMinisterMatch: No single department minister matched - the Chief Minister's office in {state} owns everything not allocated.
who.cmHoldsDept: The Chief Minister holds this department directly.
who.parallelHelp: The MLAs and MPs for {district}. Press them in parallel at any step - they answer to voters, and their office can push officials.
who.mlasLabel: MLAs - state subjects (police, schools, water, state roads)

accountability.title: Who is responsible for what?
accountability.intro: Different problems are handled by different leaders. Knowing who is in charge helps you ask the right person.
accountability.accountableLabel: You can hold them accountable for

lokSabha.title: Member of Parliament (MP) - Lok Sabha
lokSabha.summary: Your elected leader in India's Parliament, in New Delhi.
lokSabha.oneLine: Makes national laws and speaks for your area in Parliament (Delhi).
handles: Making and voting on national laws
Speaking up for your area in Parliament
Local works through the MP development fund (MPLADS)
National matters: defence, railways, highways, telecom, taxes
accountableFor: Showing up and taking part in Parliament
Using the MP development fund well for your area
notResponsible: Local roads, water, electricity, garbage - your Municipality / Panchayat
Police, state roads, government schools & hospitals - your MLA and state government
Certificates, property records, local licences - your local office

rajyaSabha.title: Member of Parliament (MP) - Rajya Sabha
rajyaSabha.summary: A leader in Parliament's upper house, chosen by state legislatures.
rajyaSabha.oneLine: Reviews national laws in Parliament's upper house.
notResponsible: Local civic issues - your local body

vidhanSabha.title: Member of Legislative Assembly (MLA)
vidhanSabha.summary: Your elected leader in the state assembly.
vidhanSabha.oneLine: Handles state matters - police, state roads, water, schools.
handles: Police, state roads, water, farming, state schools & hospitals
Representing your area in the state assembly
accountableFor: Raising your area's problems with the state
notResponsible: Day-to-day local works - often the local body

localBody.title: Municipality / Panchayat
localBody.summary: Your local council for everyday civic needs.
localBody.oneLine: Handles day-to-day local work - garbage, local roads, water, drains.
handles: Garbage, drainage, local roads, street lights
accountableFor: Everyday civic upkeep in your neighbourhood

rights.metaTitle: What are my rights? The Constitution of India in plain language
rights.metaDescription: Every right the Constitution of India gives you, explained simply - the six fundamental rights, your duties, how to enforce a right in court, and how the whole Constitution is built. With the exact article numbers.
rights.title: What are my rights?
rights.subtitle: Every person in India has rights that no government can take away. Here is what the Constitution of India actually gives you - explained simply, with the exact articles so you can always check the source.
rights.disclaimer: This is plain-language civic education, not legal advice. Each right is written into the Constitution of India; the article numbers let you check the original text.

preamble.title: The promise the Constitution makes
preamble.intro: The Constitution opens with a promise, made in the name of the people of India. It sets out what the country is meant to be, and the four values every law must serve.
preamble.quote: We, the People of India ... give to ourselves this Constitution.
preamble.identity: India is a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic.
preamble.identityNote: "Socialist" and "Secular" were added later, by the 42nd Amendment in 1976.

justice.title: Justice
justice.desc: Social, economic and political - for everyone, not just a few.

liberty.title: Liberty
liberty.desc: Of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship.

equality.title: Equality
equality.desc: Of status and of opportunity, whoever you are.

fraternity.title: Fraternity
fraternity.desc: Dignity for each person, and unity for the nation.

fundamental.title: Your six fundamental rights
fundamental.intro: These are guaranteed to you by the Constitution itself. If the state breaks them, you can go straight to court - and the court can strike down the law or the order.

equality.title: Right to Equality
equality.summary: The law treats everyone the same.
equality.means: The state cannot favour you or discriminate against you because of your religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth. Everyone is equal before the law.
examples: Equal protection of the law for every person
No discrimination in public jobs, shops, wells, roads or bathing places
Untouchability is abolished and punishable (Article 17)
The state grants no titles of nobility (Article 18)

freedom.title: Right to Freedom
freedom.summary: The core freedoms of a free citizen.
freedom.means: You can speak, gather peacefully, move and live anywhere in India, and choose your work - within reasonable limits set by law. You also have protections if you are ever arrested.
examples: Freedom of speech and expression
Assemble peacefully, form associations, move and settle anywhere in India
Practise any profession, trade or business
Protection against arbitrary arrest and detention (Article 22)

exploitation.title: Right against Exploitation
exploitation.summary: No one can be forced, bonded or trafficked.
exploitation.means: Human trafficking, begar (forced unpaid labour) and bonded labour are banned. Children under 14 cannot be made to work in factories, mines or other hazardous jobs.
examples: Human trafficking and forced labour are prohibited
Bonded labour is illegal
No child under 14 in a factory, mine or hazardous work

religion.title: Right to Freedom of Religion
religion.means: You are free to follow, practise and share any religion, or none at all. Every religious community can manage its own affairs, and India has no official state religion.
examples: Freely profess, practise and propagate your faith
Religious groups manage their own affairs and institutions
No one is forced to pay taxes for, or attend, any particular religion

cultural.title: Cultural & Educational Rights
cultural.summary: Your language and culture are protected.
cultural.means: Any community can keep its own language, script and culture. Religious and linguistic minorities can set up and run their own schools and colleges.
examples: Protect your language, script and culture
No denial of admission to a state-aided school on grounds of religion, race, caste or language
Minorities can establish and run their own educational institutions

remedies.title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
remedies.summary: The right that protects all the others.
remedies.means: If any fundamental right is violated, you can go directly to the Supreme Court, which can issue orders (writs) to enforce it. Dr B. R. Ambedkar called this the "heart and soul" of the Constitution.
examples: Move the Supreme Court directly under Article 32
Move your High Court under Article 226 (which also covers ordinary legal rights)
The court can order the state to stop acting, or to act

keyArticles.title: Life, liberty and education
keyArticles.intro: Two articles inside your Right to Freedom do the heaviest lifting in everyday life.

life.title: Right to Life and Personal Liberty
life.desc: No person can be deprived of life or personal liberty except by a fair, just and reasonable procedure established by law. Courts have read this widely - it now includes the right to live with dignity, to privacy, to a clean environment, to health, and to a livelihood.

education.title: Right to Education
education.desc: The state must provide free and compulsory education to every child aged 6 to 14. Added by the 86th Amendment in 2002 and backed by the Right to Education Act, 2009.

remedies.title: What to do if a right is broken
remedies.intro: A right you cannot enforce is only a promise. The Constitution gives you a direct route to the courts, which can issue five kinds of orders, called writs.
remedies.writsTitle: The five kinds of court order (writs)
remedies.writsIntro: A writ is simply a direct order from a court. Which one you need depends on the problem. The old Latin names are still used, but here is what each one actually does:

habeas.name: Habeas Corpus
habeas.desc: If a person is being held without a lawful reason, the court can order that they be brought before it and set free.

mandamus.plain: Make an office do its duty
mandamus.name: Mandamus
mandamus.desc: Orders a government office to do the job the law requires - for example, to issue a document you are clearly entitled to.

prohibition.plain: Stop a court that is overstepping
prohibition.name: Prohibition
prohibition.desc: Tells a lower court or tribunal to stop handling a case it has no power to hear, before it decides it.

certiorari.plain: Undo a wrong order already made
certiorari.name: Certiorari
certiorari.desc: Cancels a decision a lower court or authority has already passed without the power to, or without a fair hearing.

quo.plain: Question someone's claim to a post
quo.name: Quo Warranto
quo.desc: Challenges a person holding a public office they are not legally entitled to, and can have them removed.

practical: Free legal aid - your right if you cannot afford a lawyer (Article 39A; the National Legal Services Authority, NALSA, and district legal-services authorities)
Human Rights Commissions - national and in each state
Commissions for Women, SC / ST, Minorities and Child Rights
The Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005, to ask the government for answers
The national grievance portal (CPGRAMS) for public-service complaints

duties.title: Your fundamental duties
duties.intro: Rights come with responsibilities. The Constitution lists the duties every citizen is expected to keep - the other side of the same coin.
items: Respect the Constitution, the National Flag and the National Anthem
Cherish the ideals of the freedom struggle
Protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India
Defend the country and serve the nation when called upon
Promote harmony beyond religion, language and region, and reject practices that degrade women
Value and preserve our rich heritage and composite culture
Protect the environment - forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife - and be compassionate to living creatures
Develop a scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry
Safeguard public property and reject violence
Strive towards excellence in every sphere of activity
Provide education to your child or ward aged 6 to 14 (added in 2002)

directive.title: Goals the state must strive for
directive.intro: These are goals set for every government. Courts cannot enforce them directly, but they are fundamental to how the country should be run, and laws are shaped by them.
items: A decent livelihood and a fair sharing of resources (Article 39)
Equal pay for equal work, for women and men alike
Free legal aid, so poverty never blocks justice (Article 39A)
The right to work, to education and to help in old age, sickness and want (Articles 41-43)
A living wage and humane conditions of work
Village panchayats as units of self-government (Article 40)
Protection of the environment, forests and wildlife (Article 48A)

structure.title: How the Constitution is built
structure.intro: The Constitution of India is the longest written constitution in the world, and the supreme law of the land - every other law must obey it.
structure.partsIntro: The text is organised into parts. The ones closest to you as a citizen:
parts: Part II - Citizenship: who is an Indian citizen
Part III - Fundamental Rights: the rights on this page
Part IV - Directive Principles: goals for the state
Part IVA - Fundamental Duties: duties of citizens
Parts V-VI - the Union and the State governments
Part IX & IXA - Panchayats and Municipalities: your local government
structure.schedulesIntro: Schedules are the detailed lists attached to the Constitution. A few you may hear about:
schedules: Seventh Schedule - splits powers between the Union and the states (the Union, State and Concurrent lists)
Eighth Schedule - the 22 official languages (this site speaks all of them)
Eleventh & Twelfth Schedules - the powers of panchayats and municipalities
structure.amendDesc: The Constitution can be amended under Article 368, and has been over a hundred times. But the Supreme Court has held that its "basic structure" - such as democracy, secularism and the rule of law - can never be amended away.
structure.propertyNote: Rights can change too: the Right to Property was a fundamental right until 1978, when the 44th Amendment made it an ordinary legal right (Article 300A).

closing.title: Know who to hold to account
closing.desc: Rights are enforced by the people you elect and the officials you can reach. See who is responsible for what, then find the right office near you.
closing.sourceNote: Source: the Constitution of India. This page is for civic awareness and is not legal advice - for any specific situation, consult a lawyer or the official text.

forLeaders.metaTitle: How to be a good leader
forLeaders.metaDescription: A simple guide for anyone who leads, or wants to. Seven timeless lessons on good leadership, each from a real, named source - the Bhagavad Gita, Chanakya's Arthashastra, the Thirukkural, Emperor Ashoka and Mahatma Gandhi.
forLeaders.title: How to be a good leader
forLeaders.subtitle: This page is for you, whether you already lead or you want to one day. Long ago, India's wisest teachers wrote down how a good leader should be. Their words are gathered here in simple form, and every one is from a real, famous source.
forLeaders.address: Leading is not about being big or powerful. It is about doing good for the people who trusted you with their vote. Read these lessons slowly. They were true a thousand years ago, and they are still true today.

principles.heading: People copy their leaders
principles.quote: Whatever a great person does, common people follow. Whatever standard they set, the world pursues.
principles.explain: People watch their leaders and do the same. If you are honest and calm, people learn to be honest and calm. If you cut corners, they learn to cut corners too. So live the way you want your people to live.

principles.heading: Your people's good is your good
principles.quote: In the happiness of his people lies the ruler's happiness; in their welfare, his welfare. He should treat as good not what pleases him, but what pleases his people.
principles.explain: A leader is not happy when only he is happy. A leader is happy when the people are happy. Before every decision, ask one simple thing: does this make ordinary people's lives better? If it only helps you, drop it.

principles.heading: Be fair to everyone
principles.quote: It is not the spear but the just sceptre that gives a ruler victory, and only if it never bends.
principles.explain: A leader wins by being fair, not by being harsh or strong. Treat everyone the same, rich or poor, those who voted for you and those who did not. One unfair act can break the trust you built over many years.

principles.heading: Work for the people, not for reward
principles.quote: You have a right to your work, but never to its rewards. Do not act only for the results, and do not sit idle either.
principles.explain: Do your duty because it is right, not for money, fame or the next post. Do the work fully and honestly, and let the results come on their own.

principles.heading: First control yourself
principles.quote: Control of the senses comes from giving up greed, anger, pride and other weaknesses. Rulers who did not do this were destroyed along with their kingdoms.
principles.explain: Before you can lead others, you must lead yourself. A leader who cannot control his anger, greed or pride will not last. Stay humble and calm, especially when you have power.

principles.heading: Think of the poorest person first
principles.quote: Recall the face of the poorest and weakest person you have seen, and ask if the step you plan will be of any use to him. Will he gain anything by it? Then your doubt will melt away.
principles.explain: When a choice is hard, do not weigh it by the richest or loudest person in the room. Think of the poorest person you know, and ask: will this help that person? If yes, you are on the right path.

principles.heading: Treat every person as your own family
principles.quote: All people are my children. What I want for my own children, their welfare and happiness, I want for all people too.
principles.explain: A leader belongs to everyone, not only to his own group, religion or supporters. Care for people of every faith, language and region as if they were your own family.

forLeaders.closingTitle: The chair is for a short time. Your good work stays.
forLeaders.closingBody: One day your term ends and someone else takes the chair. What stays behind is the good you did and the lives you changed. Lead so that, even after you leave, people say: they were good for us.
forLeaders.sourcesNote: Every quote here is from a real, named and published source, and links to it. Where the words come from a holy book, we have kept the meaning true to the well-known translations.

whyCare.metaTitle: Why should I care about politics?
whyCare.metaDescription: You didn't choose to follow politics, but it already shapes your money, your street, your school and your safety. Why a few minutes of attention is worth it, what a good representative actually looks like, and how to start.
whyCare.title: Why should I care?
whyCare.subtitle: You didn't choose to follow politics. But politics already follows you - your money, your street, your child's school. Here is why a few minutes of attention is worth it, and what a good representative actually looks like.
whyCare.note: This is about paying attention, not picking a side. No parties, no verdicts - just how it touches you.

everyday.title: Government isn't far away - it is your daily life
everyday.intro: Every one of these is decided by someone you can name, elect and hold to account.

items.title: Your money
items.desc: Income tax, the GST on everything you buy, the price of petrol and a gas cylinder - all set by people in office.

items.title: Your street
items.desc: Roads, potholes, street lights, drains and clean water are somebody's job, and somebody's budget.

items.title: Your family's future
items.desc: Which government school and hospital you get - and whether they actually work - is a political choice.

items.title: Your safety
items.desc: Police, courts and how quickly an FIR gets filed depend on how the system is run and funded.

items.title: Your work
items.desc: Jobs, small-business rules, crop prices and welfare schemes are all decided, not handed down by fate.

items.title: Your voice
items.desc: Whether your area gets heard in the state capital or in Delhi depends on the person you send there.

ignore.title: What happens when nobody is watching
ignore.intro: Not caring is itself a choice - and it has a price. Usually someone else pays it, and often that someone is you.

items.title: The pressure disappears
items.desc: A representative who is never asked has little reason to show up, spend the local fund, or reply. Attention is what keeps them working.

items.title: Problems stay problems
items.desc: The pothole, the missing teacher, the dry tap - unless someone points to the right office and pushes, nothing moves on its own.

items.title: The organised get served first
items.desc: The loudest and best-connected are heard first. Everyone who looked away waits at the back of the queue.

items.title: You pay either way
items.desc: The taxes still leave your pocket and the decisions still get made. Looking away isn't free - it just costs you the say in them.

power.eyebrow: You have more than one vote
power.title: Your vote is the start, not the end
power.intro: Elections are one day in five years. Between them, you still have real levers - and most people never use them.

items.title: Vote - and vote local
items.desc: Your MLA and ward councillor shape your street more than any national vote. The smaller the election, the bigger your share of it.

items.title: Ask, on the record
items.desc: You can file a Right to Information request and make any public office answer in writing. It is your legal right, not a favour.

items.title: Knock on the right door
items.desc: Half the battle is reaching the office that actually owns the problem - not the one that is easiest to find.

items.title: Check the record
items.desc: Attendance, questions asked, funds spent: judge your representative by what they did, not by what they promised.

items.title: Speak up, together
items.desc: One voice is easy to ignore; a whole street or group raising the same issue is not. Sign, gather, show up - numbers move offices.

good.title: What does a good representative look like?
good.intro: Not their party, and not their promises - what they actually do. These are things you can check, whoever they are.

items.title: They show up
items.desc: Attendance in the house is the most basic duty of the job. Present beats absent.

items.title: They ask questions
items.desc: Raising your area's problems and questioning the government is the work itself, not a favour to you.

items.title: They spend the local fund
items.desc: The MP and MLA development funds are meant for your area - used well, not left to lapse.

items.title: They are reachable
items.desc: A real office, a working number, and someone who actually responds when you reach out.

items.title: They serve everyone
items.desc: Not only the people who voted for them, and not only their own community.

items.title: They are honest on paper
items.desc: Assets and any court cases declared truthfully in their sworn affidavit, on the public record.

items.title: They obey the same law
items.desc: The rules that bind you bind the people who make them, too.

good.note: You can check most of this yourself - a representative's attendance and spending sit on the public record. Judge them by what they do, not by what they promise.

start.title: Small habits of an alert citizen
start.intro: You don't need to become an activist. A democracy stays honest when ordinary people pay a little attention, regularly.

items.title: Know who represents you
items.desc: Learn the names of your MP, your MLA and your local councillor. You cannot hold someone to account if you don't know who they are.

items.title: Follow what they actually do
items.desc: Their attendance, the questions they raise and the money they spend are on the public record. Look past the speeches.

items.title: Vote every time - especially locally
items.desc: The smaller the election, the more your single vote weighs. Never skip a local body or panchayat vote.

items.title: Raise problems the right way
items.desc: Take an issue to the office that owns it, in writing, and escalate calmly if it is ignored. Persistence is a citizen's real power.

items.title: Don't stay silent
items.desc: Talk about local issues with family and neighbours. Awareness spreads from person to person, and a community that pays attention is hard to ignore.

closing.title: A democracy is only as awake as its people
closing.desc: Democracy does not run on election day alone. It runs on ordinary people paying attention between elections - asking, checking, and refusing to look away. That work is not for experts or activists. It is yours, and it belongs to everyone.
closing.note: Non-partisan by nature. This is about your part as a citizen - not about any party, any leader or any side.

profile.perfHelp: How well they do their official job - attendance, questions, funds. From government records.
profile.ratingHelp: What people think. This is opinion, not an official fact.
profile.performanceInsufficient: Not enough official data yet to score performance.
profile.ministerExempt: Ministers are exempt from the attendance register and answer questions rather than ask them, so there isn't enough scored data for a performance ranking yet.
profile.presidingExempt: As presiding officer of the House, they neither sign the register, table questions nor join debates as a member, so a performance ranking isn't shown.

exemptReason.minister: The House keeps no record for ministers: they are exempt from the attendance register and answer questions rather than ask them.
exemptReason.presiding-officer: Presides over the House rather than participating as a member - no record is kept.
exemptReason.no-register-record: The House keeps no signing record for this member (marked "NR" in the official register).

profile.rsDebatesNotPublished: The Rajya Sabha does not publish a member-wise debate count.
profile.stateHouseNoRecords: This State Legislature does not publish official attendance, question or debate records for its members. Missing data is shown as unavailable - it is never counted as zero.
profile.recordComingSoon: A detailed record for this leader is being added.
profile.officialInfoOnly: Appointed official - information only. Appointed officers are not rated; this page helps you reach the right person and escalate.

office.infoOnly: A non-partisan constitutional office, shown for information only. This office is never rated.
office.sourceNote: Every detail on this page is drawn from the cited public source.

profile.govRoleTitle: Role in the Government of India
profile.govRoleDesc: As {rank}, this leader runs these departments:
profile.pmRoleNote: As Prime Minister, {name} leads the entire Union government and Cabinet and oversees every ministry - not only the departments held directly, listed below.
profile.accountabilityIntro: As your representative, here is the full list of what a citizen can hold them to account for.
profile.accountabilityIntroMulti: This person holds more than one office. Here is what they are accountable for in each role.
profile.accountabilityGlanceIntro: Everything {name} answers to you for - combined across every office they hold.
profile.fullGuide: Read the full role-by-role guide (powers, limits, sources)
profile.roleSeeMore: What this role covers - and what it does not
profile.recordSubtitle: Every fact links to its official source and shows when we checked it.
profile.affidavitNote: These figures are declared by the leader in their official election form. A declared case means a trial is pending - it is not a conviction.

cases.title: Declared court cases
cases.subtitle: Cases this leader listed in their own sworn election affidavit ({asOf}), shown exactly as published by the source.
cases.none: No criminal cases were declared in this leader's election affidavit.
cases.detailUnavailable: The affidavit declares {n} case(s), but we could not re-verify the case-by-case detail from the source yet. The count above links to the original page.
cases.countMismatch: The affidavit states {declared} cases in total; the source page lists {listed} case entries. Both are shown as published.
cases.disclaimer: A pending case is an accusation before a court - it is not a conviction, and every person is presumed innocent until proven guilty. Charges under sections listed as serious in our methodology (offences affecting life, offences against women, kidnapping, robbery and similar) are highlighted so they are not missed. All details come from the leader's own affidavit via the cited source.

profile.rightToReplyTitle: Are you this leader, or spotted a mistake?
profile.rightToReplyBody: We publish only sourced facts and offer a right to reply. Tell us and we will check.
profile.shareText: I rated {name} on RankYourPolitician. See who really represents you - their record and the public rating:
profile.shareTextOfficial: See who {name} answers to on RankYourPolitician - the office, its role, and how to reach it:

ranking.unrankedHeader: Not enough verified data to rank - listed A-Z
ranking.fullSubtitle: Every leader we track, ranked by verified performance within a comparable group (same house, similar tenure). Leaders without enough published data are listed, not ranked.

trending.help: Most rated in the last 7 days. This shows attention, not a verdict.
trending.empty: Nothing trending yet - a leader appears here once they get a few ratings in a week.
trending.error: Could not load trending right now.
trending.stateHelp: Leaders from {state} with the most new ratings in the last 7 days. This shows attention, not a verdict.
trending.districtHelp: Leaders linked to {district} with the most new ratings in the last 7 days. This shows attention, not a verdict.
trending.risingAria: This week's ratings average above this leader's overall rating
trending.fallingAria: This week's ratings average below this leader's overall rating

topRated.help: Highest public rating from votes cast on this site. This is opinion, not a verdict.
topRated.empty: No one has been rated yet - be the first: open any leader's page and cast a vote.
topRated.error: Could not load top rated leaders right now.

vote.title: Rate this leader
vote.prompt: How would you rate this leader?
vote.oncePerPerson: One rating per person. You can change it later.
vote.notVerifiedNote: Ratings are people's opinion, shown separately from official facts.
vote.errorGeneric: Could not save your rating. Please try again.
vote.errorRate: Too many tries. Please wait a moment.
vote.errorBot: Please complete the human check and try again.

footer.nonpartisan: Independent and non-partisan. Not linked to any party, leader or government.
footer.disclaimer: Compiled from public and official sources for civic awareness. Not legal advice.

stateGov.title: {state} Government
stateGov.presidentsRule: This state is currently under President's Rule - there is no elected Council of Ministers.
stateGov.beingVerified: This list is being verified against official sources - please confirm with the official state government portal.
stateGov.verifyNote: Compiled from official portals and recent reporting; governments change with elections and reshuffles - please confirm current office-holders on the official state portal. Spotted an error? Use the report link on any profile.

contacts.whosWhoSub: Official {district} site - names the current officer
contacts.collectorate: District office, from the official site
contacts.sourceNote: Numbers and links are published by official government sources.
